title: `mrbsizeR`: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R
text: mrbsizeR R package based MRBSiZer method Holmström et al. (2011). name abbreviation MultiResolution Bayesian SIgnificant ZEro crossings derivatives R method extends portfolio Bayesian SiZer methods images spatial fields, originally introduced Erästö Holmström (2005). analysis spatial fields images (.e. object), scale space methods often useful. observed different scales, distinct features object can detected. Imagine concept branch tree (Lindeberg (1994)): concept makes sense scale centimeters meters. much smaller scale, one describe molecules form branch much larger scale, possible describe forest tree grows . goal scale space analysis therefore represent object different scales, done dividing family smooths. smooths made different smoothing levels smooth provides information relevant particular scale, makes possible extract scale-dependent features object. Significant Zero Crossings Derivatives (SiZer) method based scale space ideas originally developed smooths curves time series. goal find whether certain feature curve “really ” just sampling artifact. curves time series usually done investigating significance increases decreases derivatives. Within last years, concept extended various directions. contrast usual scale space procedures, wide range smooths used, mrbsizeR employs differences smooths neighboring scales. attempts separate features distinct scale categories aggressively. next step, resulting differences investigated Bayesian version SiZer see features found “really ” artifacts sampling. spherical data, Bayesian signal reconstruction implemented. analysis procedure type data therefore consists forming scale-dependent details subsequent credibility analysis. single steps application mrbsizeR explained following three examples. theory algorithm details, see Holmström et al. (2011) Schuster (2017). extensive review different statistical scale space methods including applications available Holmström Pasanen (2016).

what: Example: Data On A Regular Grid
title: `mrbsizeR`: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R
text: first example, data North American Regional Climate Change Assessment Program (NARCCAP) analyzed. NARCCAP international program producing climate change simulations Canada, United States northern Mexico. data used example based MM5I regional model simulation surface air temperature summer 1995 , see also https://www2.mmm.ucar.edu/mm5/ https://www.narccap.ucar.edu/index.html. simulation carried 120--98 regular grid, therefore 11’760 data points available total. data set part mrbsizeR package.     variables lon lat describe longitude latitude simulated surface air temperature summer 1995 degrees Celsius (su). data covers United States, southern part Canada northern part Mexico. Simulated surface air temperature summer 1995 United States, southern part Canada northern part Mexico. unit temperature degrees Celsius. output mrbsizeR analysis plots rectangular grid, makes sense display Figure also like (compare Figure ). combining Figures , still possible recognize important features coastlines, Baja California Great Lakes. Simulated surface air temperature summer 1995 United States, southern part Canada northern part Mexico rectangular grid. Red describes warmer areas, colder areas colored blue.

text: Now original signal reconstructed, one can start forming scale-depending detail components. able create differences smooths neighboring scales 𝐳1,…,𝐳L\boldsymbol{z}_{1}, \dots, \boldsymbol{z}_{L}, set appropriate smoothing levels λ1,…,λL\lambda_{1}, \dots, \lambda_{L} needs known. contrast scale space methods, wide range smoothing levels used, mrbsizeR requires . goal separate features object (: surface air temperature data) scale-distinct categories. many smoothing levels chosen, result categories feature relevant detail components. , hand, smoothing levels chosen, detail components different scales might mix recognizable anymore. therefore crucial determine smoothing levels useful, also number smoothing levels used. methods proposed selection smoothing levels one thing common: offer good starting point finding useful smoothing levels - make sure detail components captured optimally, user interaction usually inevitable. Typically, iterations necessary satisfying smoothing levels found. first method smoothing level selection depends smoother implemented mrbsizeR dimension object analyzed . plotting -called tapering functions eigenvalues 𝐐\boldsymbol{Q}, precision matrix used smoother mrbsizeR, smoothing levels λi\lambda_{}, possible determine λ\lambda’s useful. eigenvalues smoothing parameters related follows: Small λ\lambda’s involve large eigenvalues 𝐐\boldsymbol{Q}. Large λ\lambda’s involve small eigenvalues 𝐐\boldsymbol{Q}. idea plot tapering functions different ranges λ\lambda functions approximately disjoint. using λ\lambda-ranges calculating differences smooths, result orthogonal detail components. detailed information smoother used properties tapering functions, see Holmström et al. (2011) Schuster (2017). surface air temperature data simulated 120--98 grid, information passed plotting function TaperingPlot(). vector lambdaSmoother contains smoothing levels used drawing tapering functions.   corresponding tapering functions shown Figure . smoothing levels 0 ∞\infty added set smoothing levels, special meaning. Whereas smoother λ=0\lambda = 0 -called identity smoother (𝐒0𝐱=𝐱\boldsymbol{S}_{0}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{x}), smoothing λL=∞\lambda_{L} = \infty results global mean. five tapering functions Figure approximately disjoint good starting point trying find useful smoothing levels. even better starting point can attained underlying signal 𝐱\boldsymbol{x} also taken account. TaperingPlot() allows draw signal-dependent tapering functions using (optional) argument Xmu. original signal 𝐱\boldsymbol{x} unknown, replaced posterior mean.   signal-dependent tapering functions values vary wildly, visual inspection functions difficult. TaperingPlot() therefore uses moving averages absolute values tapering functions facilitate visual inspection. Figure shows disjointedness smoothed tapering functions pronounced Figure , can still observed. formal approach numerical optimization suitable objective function respect smoothing parameters. MinLambda() possible conduct optimization 2 3 λ\lambda’s. resulting smoothing levels , terms disjointedness signal-dependent tapering functions, optimal. Still, often necessary adjust smoothing levels manually. able extract also smallest-scale details can instance useful include additional, small λ\lambda. Furthermore, number “optimal” smoothing levels found MinLambda() limited. optimizing 3 λ\lambda’s, one ends sequence 5 smoothing levels total (3 optimized λ\lambda’s, λ0=0\lambda_{0} = 0, λL=∞\lambda_{L} = \infty). cases smoothing levels necessary, .e. object contains details relevant distinct scales, necessary add smoothing levels manually. However, five smoothing levels turned sufficient many cases.     arguments MinLambda() already known TaperingPlot(). addition , necessary specify nGrid (size grid optimization carried , nGrid--nGrid), nLambda (either 2 3, number λ\lambda’s optimized) sphere (logical; analysis spherical data?). optimal λ\lambda values evaluated surface air temperature data 3.16e-11, 1e+02 1e+04.     minimization result can also shown visually. optimized pair λ\lambda’s, plot drawn. Figure shows optimization surface air temperature example. optimized λ\lambda’s indices λ2\lambda_{2}, λ3\lambda_{3} λ4\lambda_{4}, λ1=0\lambda_{1} = 0 λ5=∞\lambda_{5} = \infty.   smoothing levels found different methods can now used starting point finding scale-dependent details analyzed object. Usually, iterations smoothing level adjustments necessary results found satisfying. surface air temperature example, smoothing level sequence [λ1,λ2,λ3,λ4,λ5]=[0,0.1,90,16′000,∞][\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3, \lambda_4, \lambda_5] = [0, 0.1, 90, 16'000, \infty] turned useful. Using mrbsizeRgrid(), differences smooths neighboring scales created. surface air temperature example, samples generated rmvtDCT() used input. samples already available Bayesian signal reconstruction needed, important store matrix column vector represents one sample.     resulting object tas.mrb.list containing three sublists. smMean contains mean difference smooths 𝐳\boldsymbol{z}_{} ns = 1000 samples posterior p(𝐱|𝐲)p(\boldsymbol{x}|\boldsymbol{y}). lists hpout ciout relevant posterior credibility analysis, discussed detail next subsection. smMean-plots surface air temperature example displayed Figure . increasing smoothing level λ\lambda, features getting larger. first component 𝐳1\boldsymbol{z}_{1} shows smallest scale details 𝐱\boldsymbol{x}. contours areas rapid change warmer colder temperature can observed clearly visible. 𝐳2\boldsymbol{z}_{2}, larger-scale details identifiable. Great Lakes seem colder surrounding regions areas surrounding Gulf California clearly warmer Gulf . 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3} shows Gulf California hottest region Northern America. Furthermore, Rocky Mountains identifiable cool, longish band across map. next difference smooths 𝐳4\boldsymbol{z}_{4} shows north-south temperature gradient, 𝐳5\boldsymbol{z}_{5} mean across whole map.
code: # Plot of signal-independent tapering functions TaperingPlot(lambdaSmoother = c(1, 100, 10000), mm = 120, nn = 98) # Plot of signal-dependent tapering functions TaperingPlot(lambdaSmoother = c(1, 100, 10000),               mm = 120, nn = 98, Xmu = tas.post.samp$mu) # Minimization of objective function with respect to the smoothing parameters tas.min.lambda.out <- MinLambda(Xmu = tas.post.samp$mu, mm = 120, nn = 98,                                  nLambda = 3, sphere = FALSE,                                  lambda=10^seq(-12, 10, len = 45)) # Minimal smoothing parameter values tas.min.lambda.out$lambda[tas.min.lambda.out$minind] #> [1] 3.16e-11 1.00e+02 1.00e+04 # Plot of the minimization result plot(x = tas.min.lambda.out) # Creation of differences of smooths at neighboring scales tas.mrb.out <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tas.post.samp$sample, mm = 120, nn = 98,                              lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 90, 15000), prob = 0.95) # Posterior mean of the different detail components plot(x = tas.mrb.out$smMean, color.pallet = fields::tim.colors(), turnOut = FALSE,       aspRatio = 98/120)

title: `mrbsizeR`: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R
text: detail components 𝐳\boldsymbol{z}_{} random samples posterior distribution p(𝐱|𝐲)p(\boldsymbol{x}|\boldsymbol{y}), credibility analysis done. goal infer details truly artifacts random variation. Three different methods posterior credibility analysis implemented mrbsizeR: Pointwise Maps (PW): location / pixel tested separately credibility. Highest Pointwise Probability Maps (HPW): inference done jointly locations. comparison PW maps, results non-credible areas. advantage HPW maps credible areas better connected PW maps. Small credibility islands low expressiveness appear less frequently simplify interpretation results. Simultaneous Credible Intervals (CI): Inference also done jointly locations. , simultaneous credible intervals centered posterior means calculated. CI maps flag locations credible conservatively PW HPW maps. detailed information three methods, see Holmström et al. (2011) Schuster (2017). default, credible regions three methods calculated executing mrbsizeRgrid(). corresponding lists output mrbsizeRgrid() hpout ciout.   pointwise credibility maps Figure show little credibility difference smooths 𝐳1\boldsymbol{z}_{1}. 𝐳2\boldsymbol{z}_{2}, Gulf California credibly colder Baja California Mexican mainland eastern side Gulf. 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3}, nearly everything credible. confirms Rocky Mountains really colder whole area Gulf California credibly warmer surrounding regions. 𝐳4\boldsymbol{z}_{4}, gets clear northern part map credibly colder southern part. 𝐳5\boldsymbol{z}_{5} simply shows whole global mean credible.   HPW maps Figure exhibit less credibility PW maps, especially small-scale details. However, interpretation results stays . Due joint inference locations, small islands credibility less frequent.   Detail component 𝐳1\boldsymbol{z}_{1} simultaneous credible interval maps Figure exhibit credibility. CI maps generally conservative credibility analysis method. therefore surprising details 𝐳2\boldsymbol{z}_{2} 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3} gray areas can observed PW HPW maps (compare Figures ). Still, especially larger-scale details, interpretation results change.
code: # Plot of pointwise (PW) maps plot(x = tas.mrb.out$hpout, plot_which = "PW", aspRatio = 98/120,      color = c("dodgerblue3", "gainsboro", "firebrick1"), turnOut = FALSE) # Plot of highest pointwise probability (HPW) maps plot(x = tas.mrb.out$hpout, plotWhich = "HPW", aspRatio = 98/120,      color = c("dodgerblue3", "gainsboro", "firebrick1"), turnOut = FALSE) # Plot of simultaneous credible interval (CI) maps plot(x = tas.mrb.out$ciout, color = c("dodgerblue3", "gainsboro", "firebrick1"),       turnOut = FALSE, aspRatio = 98/120)

title: `mrbsizeR`: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R
text: third example vignette demonstrates mrbsizeR can used analyze spherical data. contrast analysis data grid, Bayesian signal reconstruction implemented. form scale-dependent details, data samples need available beforehand. analysis procedure spherical data can therefore summarized two steps: Data Community Climate System Model 4.0 (CCSM4) used illustrate mrbsizeR spherical data. CCSM4 climate model simulating earth’s climate system, see Gent et al. (2011). analysis, simulated surface air temperature June years 1870–2100 considered. Instead using surface air temperature , deviation yearly mean used. detrends data makes simulations 231 consecutive years comparable. resulting data set consisting 231 observations used samples mrbsizeR analysis. data part mrbsizeR package. Figure summarizes samples mean. clearly visible temperature higher areas around equator gets lower closer Polar Regions. unit surface air temperature deviation degrees Kelvin. Deviation mean simulated air temperature measurements (CCSM4) years 1870–2100 degrees Kelvin. deviations summarized mean. conducting scale space multiresolution analysis mrbsizeR, useful smoothing parameters need evaluated first. explained NARCCAP data example, MinLambda() offers possibility find useful smoothing parameters numerically. function call spherical data nearly identical call non-spherical data, difference argument sphere TRUE.     useful smoothing levels selected, differences smooths neighboring scales can created using function mrbsizeRsphere().     creating differences smooths neighboring scales Figure , smoothing level sequence [λ1,…,λ3]=[0,0.0026,∞][\lambda_{1}, \dots, \lambda_{3}] = [0, 0.0026, \infty] used. one smoothing level added default sequence [0,∞][0, \infty]. enough capture features different scales. Whereas 𝐳1\boldsymbol{z}_{1} shows small-scale details like colder regions Tibet Chile, 𝐳2\boldsymbol{z}_{2} reveals large red-colored area around equator large blue-colored areas Polar Regions. 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3} shows global mean.   posterior credibility analysis detail component 𝐳1\boldsymbol{z}_{1} using highest pointwise probability (HPW) maps (compare Figure ) reveals regions like Chile eastern part South Africa credibly colder surrounding areas. large-scale components 𝐳2\boldsymbol{z}_{2} mostly credible hence “really ”. global mean 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3} credible example. reason data used: Instead considering yearly surface air temperature, deviations yearly mean considered. 𝐳3\boldsymbol{z}_{3} therefore average surface air temperature, average mean deviation, always equals 0.
code: # Minimization of objective function with respect to the smoothing parameters # for spherical data spherical.min.lambda.out <- MinLambda(Xmu = dat.ccsm4.mu, mm = 144, nn = 72,                                        nGrid = 35, nLambda = 2, sphere = TRUE) # Creation of differences of smooths at neighboring scales for spherical data spherical.mrb.out <- mrbsizeRsphere(posteriorFile = dat.ccsm4, mm = 144, nn = 72,                                     prob = 0.95, lambdaSmoother = c(0.0026)) # Posterior mean of the different detail components for spherical data plot(x = spherical.mrb.out$smMean, lon = dat.ccsm4$lon, lat = dat.ccsm4$lat,      color.pallet = fields::tim.colors()) # Plot of highest pointwise probability (HPW) maps for spherical data plot(x = spherical.mrb.out$hpout, lon = dat.ccsm4$lon, lat = dat.ccsm4$lat,          plotWhich = "HPW", color = c("dodgerblue3", "gainsboro", "firebrick1"))

what: What If Not Enough Computing Power Is Available?
title: `mrbsizeR`: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R
text: Especially analyses large analysis object /many samples, mrbsizeR analysis resource-intensive. cases due computational reasons enough samples can generated, additional argument smoothOut added mrbsizeRgrid() mrbsizeRsphere(). smoothOut = TRUE, output list also contain sublist smoothSamples, includes differences smooths samples. makes possible manually increase number samples get HPW maps CI maps higher confidence. example provided following code chunk.
code: # Generate samples from posterior distribution tas.post.samp <- rmvtDCT(object = tas.su.1995.MM5I$su,                           lambda = 0.2, sigma = 36, nu0 = 15, ns = 1000)  # Do mrbsizeR analysis and output the differences of smooths for all samples tas.mrb.out.1 <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tas.post.samp$sample, mm = 120,                                nn = 98, lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 90, 15000),                               prob = 0.95, smoothOut = TRUE)  # Do the same procedure again tas.post.samp <- rmvtDCT(object = tas.su.1995.MM5I$su,                           lambda = 0.2, sigma = 36, nu0 = 15, ns = 1000)  tas.mrb.out.2 <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tas.post.samp$sample, mm = 120,                                nn = 98, lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 90, 15000),                                prob = 0.95, smoothOut = TRUE)  # Combine all differences-of-smooths-samples and call CImap manually smoothSamples <- list(); ciout <- list()  for(i in 1:length(tas.mrb.out.1$smoothSamples)) {   smoothSamples <- cbind(tas.mrb.out.1$smoothSamples[[i]],                           tas.mrb.out.2$smoothSamples[[i]])   ciout[[i]] <- CImap(smoothVec = smoothSamples, mm = 120, nn = 98, prob = 0.95) }  # Set the class correctly for visualizing the output. # Titles need to be defined in this case! # Class name CI maps: "CImapGrid" or "CImapSphere" # Class name PW / HPW maps: "HPWmapGrid" or "HPWmapSphere" class(ciout) <- "CImapGrid" plot(ciout, title = c("Diff_1", "Diff_2", "Diff_3", "Diff_4", "Diff_5"))

title: Authors and Citation
text: Schuster T, Flury R (2024). mrbsizeR: Scale Space Multiresolution Analysis Random Signals. R package version 1.3.1, https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=mrbsizeR.
code: @Manual{,   title = {mrbsizeR: Scale Space Multiresolution Analysis of Random Signals},   author = {Thimo Schuster and Roman Flury},   year = {2024},   note = {R package version 1.3.1},   url = {https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=mrbsizeR}, }

title: Scale Space Multiresolution Analysis of Random Signals
text: preferred way install mrbsizeR CRAN : can install mrbsizeR devel version github :
code: install.packages("mrbsizeR") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("romanflury/mrbsizeR")

title: Scale Space Multiresolution Analysis of Random Signals
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Generate samples from multivariate t-distribution tSamp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 0.2, sigma = 6, nu0 = 15,                   ns = 1000)   # mrbsizeRgrid analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tSamp$sample, mm = 10, nn = 10,                        lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)  # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, turn_out = TRUE)  # Credibility analysis using simultaneous credible intervals plot(x = mrbOut$ciout, turn_out = TRUE)

what: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
text: Simultaneous credible intervals differences smooths neighboring scales \(z_{}\) computed.

title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
code: CImap(smoothVec, mm, nn, prob = 0.95)

title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
text: smoothVec Differences smooths neighboring scales. mm Number rows original input object. nn Number columns original input object. prob Credibility level posterior credibility analysis. default prob = 0.95.

title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
text: array simultaneous credible intervals VmapCI     dimensions original input object, mm nn.

title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
text: CImap internal function mrbsizeRgrid usually used independently. output can analyzed plotting function plot.CImapGrid.

title: Computation of simultaneous credible intervals. — CImap
code: # Artificial sample data: 10 observations (5-by-2 object), 10 samples set.seed(987) sampleData  <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData [4:6, ] <- sampleData [4:6, ] + 5  # Calculation of the simultaneous credible intervals CImap(smoothVec = sampleData , mm = 5, nn = 2, prob = 0.95) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]  0.5  1.0 #> [2,]  0.5  0.5 #> [3,]  0.5  0.5 #> [4,]  1.0  0.5 #> [5,]  1.0  0.5

what: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
text: discrete cosine transform (DCT) matrix given dimension n calculated.

title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
code: dctMatrix(n)

title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
text: n Dimension DCT matrix.

title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
text: n--n DCT matrix.

title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
text: function can used 1D- 2D-DCT transforms data. 1D: Let Q m--n matrix data. D m--m DCT matrix created dctMatrix(m). D %*% Q returns discrete cosine transform columns Q. t(D) %*% Q returns inverse DCT columns Q. D orthogonal, solve(D) = t(D). 2D: Let Q m--n matrix data. D_m m--m DCT matrix created dctMatrix(m), D_n n--n DCT matrix created dctMatrix(n). D_m %*% Q %*% t(D_n) computes 2D-DCT Q. inverse 2D-DCT Q can computed via t(D_mm) %*% DCT_Q %*% D_n. D_m transforms along columns, D_n along rows. Since D orthogonal, solve(D) = t(D). can faster use dctMatrix using direct transformation, especially calculating several DCT's.

title: Create a n-by-n discrete cosine transform matrix. — dctMatrix
code: D <- dctMatrix(5)

what: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
text: fftshift R equivalent Matlab function fftshift applied matrices. information fftshift see Matlab documentation.

title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
code: fftshift(inputMatrix, dimension = -1)

title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
text: inputMatrix Matrix swapped. dimension swap performed? 1: swap halves along rows. 2: swap halves along columns. -1: swap first quadrant third second quadrant fourth.

title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
text: Swapped matrix.

title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
text: possible swap halves quadrants input matrix. Halves can swapped along rows (dimension = 1) along columns (dimension = 2). swapping quadrants, fftshift swaps first quadrant third second quadrant fourth (dimension = -1).

title: Swap the quadrants or halves of a 2d matrix. — fftshift
code: set.seed(987)  sampleMat <- matrix(sample(1:10, size = 25, replace = TRUE), nrow = 5)  # Swap halves along the rows: fftshift(sampleMat, dimension = 1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    2    2    5   10   10 #> [2,]    8    8   10    8    8 #> [3,]    9    1    4    3    9 #> [4,]    9    1    2    9    9 #> [5,]    7    3    2    3    3  # Swap halves along the columns: fftshift(sampleMat, dimension = 2) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    9    9    1    4 #> [2,]    9    9    9    1    2 #> [3,]    3    3    7    3    2 #> [4,]   10   10    2    2    5 #> [5,]    8    8    8    8   10  # Swap first quadrant with third and second quadrant with fourth: fftshift(sampleMat, dimension = -1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]   10   10    2    2    5 #> [2,]    8    8    8    8   10 #> [3,]    3    9    9    1    4 #> [4,]    9    9    9    1    2 #> [5,]    3    3    7    3    2

what: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
text: Pointwise (PW) probabilities highest pointwise (HPW) probabilities differences smooths neighboring scales computed.

title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
code: HPWmap(smoothVec, mm, nn, prob = 0.95)

title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
text: smoothVec Differences smooths neighboring scales. mm Number rows original input image. nn Number columns original input image. prob Credibility level posterior credibility analysis

title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
text: List two arrays: pw: Pointwise probabilities (VmapPW) including dimensions     original input image, mm nn. hpw: Highest pointwise probabilities (VmapHPW) including     dimensions original input image, mm nn.

title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
text: HPWmap internal function mrbsizeRgrid usually used independently. output can analyzed plotting function plot.HPWmapGrid.

title: Computation of pointwise and highest pointwise probabilities. — HPWmap
code: # Artificial sample data: 10 observations (5-by-2 object), 10 samples set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, ] <- sampleData[4:6, ] + 5  # Calculation of the simultaneous credible intervals HPWmap(smoothVec = sampleData, mm = 5, nn = 2, prob = 0.95) #> $pw #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]  0.5  1.0 #> [2,]  0.5  0.5 #> [3,]  0.5  0.5 #> [4,]  1.0  0.5 #> [5,]  1.0  0.5 #>  #> $hpw #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]  0.5  1.0 #> [2,]  0.5  0.5 #> [3,]  0.5  0.5 #> [4,]  1.0  0.5 #> [5,]  1.0  0.5 #>

what: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
text: ifftshift R equivalent Matlab function ifftshift applied matrices. information ifftshift see Matlab documentation.

title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
code: ifftshift(inputMatrix, dimension = -1)

title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
text: inputMatrix Matrix swapped. dimension swap performed? 1: swap halves along rows. 2: swap halves along columns. -1: swap first quadrant third second quadrant fourth.

title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
text: Swapped matrix.

title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
text: ifftshift inverse function fftshift. information see details fftshift

title: Inverse FFT shift of a 2d matrix. — ifftshift
code: set.seed(987) sampleMat <- matrix(sample(1:10, size = 25, replace = TRUE), nrow = 5)  # Swap halves along the rows: ifftshift(sampleMat, dimension = 1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    3    2    3    3 #> [2,]    2    2    5   10   10 #> [3,]    8    8   10    8    8 #> [4,]    9    1    4    3    9 #> [5,]    9    1    2    9    9  # Swap halves along the columns: ifftshift(sampleMat, dimension = 2) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    4    3    9    9    1 #> [2,]    2    9    9    9    1 #> [3,]    2    3    3    7    3 #> [4,]    5   10   10    2    2 #> [5,]   10    8    8    8    8  # Swap first quadrant with third and second quadrant with fourth: ifftshift(sampleMat, dimension = -1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    2    3    3    7    3 #> [2,]    5   10   10    2    2 #> [3,]   10    8    8    8    8 #> [4,]    4    3    9    9    1 #> [5,]    2    9    9    9    1

what: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
text: Numerical optimization objective function \(G\) carried find appropriate signal-dependent smoothing levels (\(\lambda\)'s). easier visual inspection via signal-dependent tapering function TaperingPlot.

title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
code: MinLambda(Xmu, mm, nn, nGrid, nLambda = 2, lambda, sphere = FALSE)

title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
text: Xmu Posterior mean input object vector. mm Number rows original input object. nn Number columns original input object. nGrid Size grid objective function evaluated (nGrid--nGrid). argument ignorded sequence lambda specified. nLambda Number lambdas minimize . Possible arguments: 2 (default) 3. lambda \(\lambda\)-sequence used optimization. nothing provided, lambda <- 10^seq(-3, 10, len = nGrid) used data grid lambda <- 10^seq(-6, 1, len = nGrid) used spherical data. sphere TRUE FALSE: input object defined sphere?

title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
text: list 3 objects: G Value objective function \(G\). lambda Evaluated smoothing parameters \(\lambda\). minind Index minimal \(\lambda\)'s. lambda[minind]     gives minimal values.

title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
text: signal-dependent tapering functions quiet irregular, hard find appropriate smoothing values visual inspection tapering function plot. formal approach numerical optimization objective function. Optimization can carried 2 3 smoothing parameters. smoothing parameters 0 \(\infty\) always added, results mrbsizeR analysis 4 5 smoothing parameters. Sometimes, features input object can extracted using smoothing levels proposed MinLambda. might necessary include additional smoothing levels. plot.minLambda creates plot objective function \(G\) grid. minimum indicated white point. minimum values \(\lambda\)'s can extracted output MinLambda, see examples.

title: Numerical optimization for finding appropriate smoothing levels. — MinLambda
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Minimization of two lambdas on a 20-by-20-grid minlamOut <- MinLambda(Xmu = c(sampleData), mm = 10, nn = 10,                         nGrid = 20, nLambda = 2)  # Minimal lambda values minlamOut$lambda[minlamOut$minind] #> [1]   0.1128838 297.6351442

what: mrbsizeR: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R. — mrbsizeR-package
title: mrbsizeR: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R. — mrbsizeR-package
text: mrbsizeR contains method scale space multiresolution analysis spatial fields images capture scale-dependent features. name abbreviation MultiResolution Bayesian SIgnificant ZEro crossings derivatives R method combines concept statistical scale space analysis Bayesian SiZer method.

title: mrbsizeR: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R. — mrbsizeR-package
text: mrbsizeR analysis can applied data regular grid spherical data. data grid, scale space multiresolution analysis three steps: Bayesian signal reconstruction. Using differences smooths, scale-dependent features reconstructed signal found. Posterior credibility analysis differences smooths created. first step, Bayesian signal reconstruction used extract underlying signal potentially noisy observation. Samples resulting posterior can generated used analysis. finding features different scales, differences smooths neighboring scales used. important distinction scale space methods (usually use wide range smoothing levels without taking differences) tries separate features distinct scale categories aggressively. successful extraction scale-different features, posterior credibility analysis necessary assess whether features found “really ” artifacts random sampling. spherical data, Bayesian signal reconstruction implemented mrbsizer. Data samples therefore need available beforehand. analysis procedure can therefore summarized two steps: Using differences smooths, scale-dependent features reconstructed signal found. Posterior credibility analysis differences smooths created. method first proposed Holmstrom, Pasanen, Furrer, Sain (2011). Major Functions TaperingPlot Graphical estimation useful smoothing       levels. Can used signal-independent signal-dependent. MinLambda Numerical estimation useful smoothing levels.       Takes underlying signal account. plot.minLambda can       used plotting result. rmvtDCT Creates samples regular grid       multivariate \(t_{\nu}\)-distribution using discrete cosine transform (DCT). mrbsizeRgrid Interface mrbsizeR method data       regular grid. Differences smooths neighboring scales created       posterior credibility analysis conducted. results can       visualized using plot.smMeanGrid,plot.HPWmapGrid       plot.CImapGrid. mrbsizeRsphere Interface mrbsizeR method data       sphere. Differences smooths neighboring scales created       posterior credibility analysis conducted. results can       visualized using plot.smMeanSphere,plot.HPWmapSphere       plot.CImapSphere.       data sphere, Bayesian signal reconstruction implemented.       Samples provided instead. Getting Started vignette package offers extensive overview functionality usage mrbsizeR. References Holmstrom, L. Pasanen, L. (2011). MRBSiZer. Holmstrom, L., Pasanen, L., Furrer, R., Sain, S. R. (2011).    Scale space multiresolution analysis random signals.    Computational Statistics Data Analysis, 55, 2840-2855.    <DOI:10.1016/j.csda.2011.04.011>. Holmstrom, L. Pasanen, L. (2016). Statistical scale space methods.    International Statistical Review. <DOI:10.1111/insr.12155>. DISCLAIMER: author can guarantee correctness function program package.

title: mrbsizeR: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R. — mrbsizeR-package
text: Maintainer: Roman Flury fluryro@gmail.com Authors: Thimo Schuster thimo.schuster@gmail.com contributors: Leena Pasanen leena.pasanen@oulu.fi [contributor] Reinhard Furrer reinhard.furrer@math.uzh.ch [contributor]

title: mrbsizeR: Scale space multiresolution analysis in R. — mrbsizeR-package
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Generate samples from multivariate t-distribution tSamp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 0.2, sigma = 6, nu0 = 15,                   ns = 1000)  # mrbsizeRgrid analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tSamp$sample, mm = 10, nn = 10,                        lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)  # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, turn_out = TRUE) #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter   # Credibility analysis using simultaneous credible intervals plot(x = mrbOut$ciout, turn_out = TRUE) #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter #> Warning: "turn_out" is not a graphical parameter

what: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
text: mrbsizeRgrid interface scale space multiresolution method data regular grid. , differences smooths well posterior credibility analysis computed. output can analyzed plotting functions plot.smMeanGrid, plot.CImapGrid plot.HPWmapGrid.

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
code: mrbsizeRgrid(   posteriorFile,   mm,   nn,   lambdaSmoother,   prob = 0.95,   smoothOut = FALSE )

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
text: posteriorFile Matrix posterior samples column vectors. mm Number rows original object. nn Number columns original object. lambdaSmoother Vector consisting smoothing levels used. prob Credibility level posterior credibility analysis. smoothOut differences smooths neighboring scales returned output (FALSE default)?

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
text: list containing following sublists: smMean Posterior mean differences smooths created. hpout Pointwise (PW) highest pointwise (HPW) probabilities     differences smooths created. ciout Simultaneous credible intervals (CI) differences     smooths created. smoothSamples Samples differences smooths neighboring scales,     column vectors.

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
text: mrbsizeRgrid conducts two steps scale space multiresolution analysis: Extraction scale-dependent features reconstructed signal.         done smoothing different smoothing levels taking         difference smooths neighboring scales. Posterior credibility analysis differences smooths created.         Three different methods applied: Pointwise probabilities (see HPWmap),         highest pointwise probabilities (see HPWmap) simultaneous         credible intervals (see CImap). signal can reconstructed using build-multivariate t-distribution sampling rmvtDCT. also possible provide samples generated methods, see parameter posteriorFile examples. information examples, see vignette.

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals. — mrbsizeRgrid
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Generate samples from multivariate t-distribution tSamp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 0.2, sigma = 6, nu0 = 15,                   ns = 1000)     # mrbsizeRgrid analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tSamp$sample, mm = 10, nn = 10,                         lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)

text: posteriorFile Matrix posterior samples column vectors. mm Number rows original object. nn Number columns original object. lambdaSmoother Vector consisting smoothing levels used. prob Credibility level posterior credibility analysis. smoothOut differences smooths neighboring scales returned output (FALSE default)?

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals for spherical data. — mrbsizeRsphere
text: list containing following sublists: smMean Posterior mean differences smooths created. hpout Pointwise (PW) highest pointwise (HPW) probabilities    differences smooths created. ciout Simultaneous credible intervals (CI) differences     smooths created. smoothSamples Samples differences smooths neighboring scales,     column vectors.

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals for spherical data. — mrbsizeRsphere
text: contrast mrbsizeRgrid, mrbsizeRsphere conduct Bayesian signal reconstruction via sampling posterior distribution. Samples posterior distribution provided instead. information examples, see mrbsizeRgrid vignette.

title: Multiresolution analysis of random signals for spherical data. — mrbsizeRsphere
code: # Artificial spherical sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(2000), nrow = 200) sampleData[50:65, ] <- sampleData[50:65, ] + 5  # mrbsizeRsphere analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRsphere(posteriorFile = sampleData, mm = 10, nn = 20,                             lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)

what: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
text: Maps simultaneous credible intervals differences smooths neighboring scales \(z_{}\) plotted.

title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
code: # S3 method for class 'CImapGrid' plot(   x,   color = c("firebrick1", "gainsboro", "dodgerblue3"),   turnOut = TRUE,   title,   aspRatio = 1,   ... )

title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
text: x List containing simultaneous credible intervals differences smooths. color Vector length 3 containing colors used credibility maps. first color represents credibly negative pixels, second color pixels credibly different zero third color credibly positive pixels. turnOut Logical. output images turned 90 degrees counter-clockwise? title Vector containing one string per plot. required number titles equal length(mrbOut$ciout). title passed, defaults used. aspRatio Adjust aspect ratio plots. default aspRatio = 1 produces square plots. ... graphical parameters can passed.

title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
text: Plots simultaneous credible intervals differences     smooths created.

title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
text: default colors maps following meaning: Blue: Credibly positive pixels. Red: Credibly negative pixels. Grey: Pixels credibly different zero. x corresponds ciout-part output mrbsizeRgrid.

title: Plot of simultaneous credible intervals. — plot.CImapGrid
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Generate samples from multivariate t-distribution tSamp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 0.2, sigma = 6, nu0 = 15,                    ns = 1000)     # mrbsizeRgrid analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tSamp$sample, mm = 10, nn = 10,                         lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)  # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, turnOut = TRUE)                       # Credibility analysis using simultaneous credible intervals plot(x = mrbOut$ciout, turnOut = TRUE)

title: Plotting of simultaneous credible intervals on a sphere. — plot.CImapSphere
code: # Artificial spherical sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(2000), nrow = 200) sampleData[50:65, ] <- sampleData[50:65, ] + 5 lon <- seq(-180, 180, length.out = 20) lat <- seq(-90, 90, length.out = 10)  # mrbsizeRsphere analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRsphere(posteriorFile = sampleData, mm = 20, nn = 10,                           lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 1), prob = 0.95)                             # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, lon = lon, lat = lat,      color = fields::tim.colors())    # Credibility analysis using simultaneous credible intervals plot(x = mrbOut$ciout, lon = lon, lat = lat)

code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Generate samples from multivariate t-distribution tSamp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 0.2, sigma = 6, nu0 = 15,                    ns = 1000)  # mrbsizeRgrid analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRgrid(posteriorFile = tSamp$sample, mm = 10, nn = 10,                        lambdaSmoother = c(1, 1000), prob = 0.95)  # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, turnOut = TRUE)   # Credibility analysis using pointwise (PW) maps plot(x = mrbOut$hpout, plotWhich = "PW", turnOut = TRUE)   # Credibility analysis using highest pointwise probability (HPW) maps plot(x = mrbOut$hpout, plotWhich = "HPW", turnOut = TRUE)

code: # Artificial spherical sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(2000), nrow = 200) sampleData[50:65, ] <- sampleData[50:65, ] + 5 lon <- seq(-180, 180, length.out = 20) lat <- seq(-90, 90, length.out = 10)  # mrbsizeRsphere analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRsphere(posteriorFile = sampleData, mm = 20, nn = 10,                           lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 1), prob = 0.95)                             # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, lon = lon, lat = lat,      color = fields::tim.colors())    # Credibility analysis using pointwise (PW) maps plot(x = mrbOut$hpout, lon = lon, lat = lat, plotWhich = "PW")   # Credibility analysis using highest pointwise probability (HPW) maps plot(x = mrbOut$hpout, lon = lon, lat = lat, plotWhich = "HPW")

what: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
text: Scale-dependent features plotted using differences smooths neighboring scales. features summarized posterior mean. Continental lines added plots.

title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
code: # S3 method for class 'smMeanSphere' plot(   x,   lon,   lat,   color.pallet = fields::tim.colors(),   turnOut = TRUE,   title,   ... )

title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
text: x List containing posterior mean differences smooths. lon Vector containing longitudes data points. lat Vector containing latitudes data points. color.pallet color pallet used plotting scale-dependent features. turnOut Logical. output images turned 90 degrees counter-clockwise? title Vector containing one string per plot. required number titles equal length(mrbOut$smMean). title passed, defaults used. ... graphical parameters can passed.

title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
text: Plots differences smooths created.

title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
text: x corresponds smmean-part output mrbsizeRsphere.

title: Plotting of scale-dependent features on a sphere. — plot.smMeanSphere
code: # Artificial spherical sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(2000), nrow = 200) sampleData[50:65, ] <- sampleData[50:65, ] + 5 lon <- seq(-180, 180, length.out = 20) lat <- seq(-90, 90, length.out = 10)  # mrbsizeRsphere analysis mrbOut <- mrbsizeRsphere(posteriorFile = sampleData, mm = 20, nn = 10,                           lambdaSmoother = c(0.1, 1), prob = 0.95)                             # Posterior mean of the differences of smooths plot(x = mrbOut$smMean, lon = lon, lat = lat,      color = fields::tim.colors(), turnOut = FALSE)

what: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
text: Samples marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution normal-inverse-chi-squared-prior generated.

title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
code: rmvtDCT(object, lambda, sigma, nu0, ns)

title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
text: object Observed object, matrix. lambda Scaling parameter (\(\lambda\)) normal-inverse-chi-squared-prior. sigma Square root \(\sigma_{0}^{2}\) parameter normal-inverse-chi-squared-prior. nu0 Degrees freedom (\(\nu_{0}\)) normal-inverse-chi-square-prior. ns Number samples generated.

title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
text: list containing following elements: sample Samples marginal posterior input. mu Mean marginal posterior input.

title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
text: eigenvalue decomposition used sampling. speed computations, 2D discrete cosine transform (DCT) implemented, see dctMatrix. output list containing Samples marginal posterior input column vectors. mean marginal posterior input vector.

title: Sampling from marginal posterior multivariate t-distribution. — rmvtDCT
code: # Artificial sample data set.seed(987) sampleData <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleData[4:6, 6:8] <- sampleData[4:6, 6:8] + 5  # Sampling from a multivariate t-distribution t_dist_samp <- rmvtDCT(object = sampleData, lambda = 1, sigma = 10,                        nu0 = 50, ns = 1000)

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
text: Tapering functions corresponding smoothing levels lambdaSmoother drawn. plot helps assess chosen smoothing levels appropriate.

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
code: TaperingPlot(lambdaSmoother, mm, nn, Xmu, returnseq = FALSE, ...)

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
text: lambdaSmoother Vector consisting smoothing levels used. mm Number rows original input object. nn Number columns original input object. Xmu availabe, posterior mean input object. returnseq instead plotting return tapering sequences. ... graphical parameters can passed.

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
text: Plots tapering functions differences smooths     neighboring scales created.

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
text: tapering functions smoothing levels chosen generally approximately disjoint. produce features somewhat orthogonal. orthogonal features, likely difference smooths corresponds different pattern input image. Sometimes, patterns input image can extracted using smoothing levels whose tapering functions disjoint. might necessary include additional smoothing levels, disjointedness might satisfied anymore. selection appropriate smoothing levels method therefore requires user interaction. Still, choosing disjoint tapering functions finding appropriate smoothing levels good starting point. Better results obtained structure posterior mean input also taken account. posterior mean available, can added argument Xmu moving averages absolute values signal-dependent tapering functions drawn. MinLambda offers formal approach optimizing disjointedness tapering functions can help finding appropriate smoothing levels input signal taken account.

title: Plot of tapering functions. — TaperingPlot
code: # Signal-independent tapering function plot for a 30-by-10 object with  # the smoothing parameter sequence [0, 1, 10, 1000, inf]:   TaperingPlot(lambdaSmoother = c(1, 10, 1000), mm = 30, nn = 10)    # Signal-dependent tapering function plot for a 30-by-10 object with  # the smoothing parameter sequence [0, 1, 10, 1000, inf]:   set.seed(987) xmuExample <- c(stats::rnorm(300)) TaperingPlot(lambdaSmoother = c(1, 10, 1000), mm = 30, nn = 10,               Xmu = xmuExample)

title: Generate a tridiagonal matrix. — tridiag
text: Generate tridiagonal matrix upperDiag superdiagonal, lowerDiag subdiagonal mainDiag diagonal.

title: Generate a tridiagonal matrix. — tridiag
code: tridiag(mainDiag, upperDiag, lowerDiag)

title: Generate a tridiagonal matrix. — tridiag
text: mainDiag Diagonal tridiagonal matrix. upperDiag Superdiagonal tridiagonal matrix. Must length length(mainDiag) - 1. lowerDiag Subdiagonal tridiagonal matrix. Must length length(mainDiag) - 1.

title: Generate a tridiagonal matrix. — tridiag
text: Tridiagonal matrix.

title: Generate a tridiagonal matrix. — tridiag
code: set.seed(987) mainDiag <- sample(100:110, size = 6, replace = TRUE) upperDiag <- sample(10:20, size = 5, replace = TRUE) lowerDiag <- sample(1:10, size = 5, replace = TRUE)  tridiag(mainDiag, upperDiag, lowerDiag)   #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] [,6] #> [1,]  108   10    0    0    0    0 #> [2,]    2  108   12    0    0    0 #> [3,]    0    2  106   11    0    0 #> [4,]    0    0    5  101   17    0 #> [5,]    0    0    0   10  107   13 #> [6,]    0    0    0    0    3  100

title: Turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. — turnmat
text: Help function turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. turnmat used internal function plotting functions. Depending data stored, can necessary turn matrices 90 degrees counter-clockwise able plot correctly.

title: Turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. — turnmat
code: turnmat(x)

title: Turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. — turnmat
text: x Matrix turned.

title: Turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. — turnmat
text: Matrix x, turned 90 degrees counter-clockwise.

title: Turn matrix 90 degrees counter-clockwise. — turnmat
code: set.seed(987) sampleMat <- matrix(stats::rnorm(100), nrow = 10) sampleMatTurn <- turnmat(x = sampleMat)
